frames.caption: Start with an empty operand stack and scan the tokens left to right.
frames.note: The stack holds operands still waiting to be consumed. In valid postfix, every operator finds its operands already on top, so a single linear pass evaluates the whole expression — O(n) time, O(n) space for the stack.

frames.caption: '2' is a number → push 2 onto the stack.
frames.note: A number is an operand with no work to do yet — it just waits to be consumed by a later operator. We push it so the most recently seen operands sit on top, ready to be grabbed. Postfix is why this is so clean: operands always appear before the operator that uses them, so by the time we hit an operator its inputs are already the top of the stack.

frames.caption: '1' is a number → push 1 onto the stack.
frames.note: A number is an operand with no work to do yet — it just waits to be consumed by a later operator. We push it so the most recently seen operands sit on top, ready to be grabbed. Postfix is why this is so clean: operands always appear before the operator that uses them, so by the time we hit an operator its inputs are already the top of the stack.

frames.caption: '+' is an operator → pop the two most recent operands.
frames.note: Reaching an operator means its operands are already on the stack (postfix guarantees operands precede their operator). We will pop two of them now.

frames.caption: First pop → b = 1 (the RIGHT operand).
frames.note: The first value popped is the right operand. Because it was pushed most recently, it is the closest operand to this operator — exactly the right-hand side of the operation.

frames.caption: Second pop → a = 2 (the LEFT operand).
frames.note: The next value popped is the left operand. Now we have a and b in the correct roles, so we can compute a OP b — order is what makes - and / come out right.

frames.caption: Compute 2 + 1 = 3 and push it back.
frames.note: An operator consumes exactly the two most recent operands. We pop twice: the FIRST pop is the right operand, the SECOND pop is the left, so we compute left OP right (order matters for - and /). Push the single result back, and it becomes an operand for whatever comes next. This LIFO consume-and-replace is precisely why postfix needs no parentheses and no precedence rules — evaluation order is already baked into the token order.

frames.caption: '3' is a number → push 3 onto the stack.
frames.note: A number is an operand with no work to do yet — it just waits to be consumed by a later operator. We push it so the most recently seen operands sit on top, ready to be grabbed. Postfix is why this is so clean: operands always appear before the operator that uses them, so by the time we hit an operator its inputs are already the top of the stack.

frames.caption: '*' is an operator → pop the two most recent operands.
frames.note: Reaching an operator means its operands are already on the stack (postfix guarantees operands precede their operator). We will pop two of them now.

frames.caption: First pop → b = 3 (the RIGHT operand).
frames.note: The first value popped is the right operand. Because it was pushed most recently, it is the closest operand to this operator — exactly the right-hand side of the operation.

frames.caption: Second pop → a = 3 (the LEFT operand).
frames.note: The next value popped is the left operand. Now we have a and b in the correct roles, so we can compute a OP b — order is what makes - and / come out right.

frames.caption: Compute 3 * 3 = 9 and push it back.
frames.note: An operator consumes exactly the two most recent operands. We pop twice: the FIRST pop is the right operand, the SECOND pop is the left, so we compute left OP right (order matters for - and /). Push the single result back, and it becomes an operand for whatever comes next. This LIFO consume-and-replace is precisely why postfix needs no parentheses and no precedence rules — evaluation order is already baked into the token order.

frames.caption: Tokens exhausted; one value remains → return 9.
frames.note: A well-formed postfix expression collapses to exactly one operand: the answer. Each token was handled once (a push, or a pop-pop-push), confirming the O(n) bound. For ["2","1","+","3","*"]: push 2, push 1, + → 3, push 3, * → 9.
